summary: Gruber discusses the design decision behind Markdown's three unordered list markers (asterisk, hyphen, and plus), and shares Twitter poll results on which people actually use. Hyphens won with 54%, asterisks got 42%, and plus was nearly ignored at 4%. He reflects that he himself uses all three depending on mood, which is partly why Markdown supports all three — he couldn't pick just one in 2003. He also notes the regrettable omission of the actual bullet character, which was left out due to character-encoding concerns that are now largely resolved thanks to UTF-8 adoption.
key_points: Markdown supports three unordered list characters (*, -, +) because Gruber wanted to let people choose what feels natural, and he couldn't pick just one himself
A Twitter poll showed hyphens are the most popular (54%), followed by asterisks (42%), with plus nearly unused (4%)
The actual bullet character (•) was omitted from Markdown because character-encoding mismatches were a daily problem in 2003, though UTF-8 has since made this a non-issue
Gruber notes many of his followers use third-party Twitter clients and couldn't vote in the poll, but their replies matched the same distribution
money_quotes: Why all three? More or less: why not?
Part of the reason Markdown supports all three characters is that I couldn't decide on just one back in 2003, and I still can't.
Because a lot of my followers have the good taste to use third-party clients like Tweetbot and Twitterrific, I wound up getting a lot of 'responses' by way of replies to my tweet.
Lots of fans of asterisks and hyphens, crickets chirping for plus.
Today, UTF-8 is sufficiently universal that using such characters in an update to Markdown would probably work out fine.
key_insight: Markdown's support for three list marker characters reflects a deliberate design philosophy of accommodating user preference over enforcing a single convention, though the omission of the literal bullet character is a regret now remedied by universal UTF-8 adoption.
